elements.sent-2411-001: An analyst observes the frequency with which a decision maker (\dm) takes actions, but does not observe the frequency of actions conditional on the payoff-relevant state.
elements.sent-2411-002: We ask when can the analyst rationalize the \dm's choices as if the \dm\ first learns something about the state before taking action.
elements.sent-2411-003: We provide a support function characterization of the triples of utility functions, prior beliefs, and (marginal) distributions over actions such that the \dm's action distribution is consistent with information given the agent's prior and utility function.
elements.sent-2411-004: Assumptions on the cardinality of the state space and the utility function allow us to refine this characterization, obtaining a sharp system of finitely many inequalities the utility function, prior, and action distribution must satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-005: We apply our characterization to study comparative statics and ring-network games, and to identify conditions under which a data set is consistent with a public information structure in first-order Bayesian persuasion games.
elements.sent-2411-006: We characterize the set of distributions over posterior beliefs that are consistent with the \dm's choices.
elements.sent-2411-007: Assuming the first-order approach applies, we extend our results to settings with a continuum of actions and/or states.
elements.sent-2411-008: JEL codes: D44, D82, D83 Keywords: revealed preference, revealed information, distributions with given marginals, support function, stochastic choice out of menus, state-dependent stochastic choice, information design, Bayesian persuasion, flows in networks, optimal transport.
elements.sent-2411-009: Many decisions depend on agents' information about factors unknown to them, and yet researchers rarely observe this information.
elements.sent-2411-010: Absent data on information, researchers oftentimes impose assumptions on how agents form expectations, or on the information available to them.
elements.sent-2411-011: Recognizing the impact these assumptions have on model predictions and parameter estimates, a recent empirical literature seeks to obtain preference estimates in a way that is robust to the information possessed by the agents.
elements.sent-2411-012: For instance, \cite{dickstein2018exporters , \cite*{dickstein2024patient , \cite{gualdani2019identification , and \cite{rambachan2022identifying develop identification and estimation results to understand the role of information in firms' decisions to export, physicians treatment recommendations, voters decisions to vote for a particular candidate, and whether agents make systematic prediction mistakes, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Similar methods and questions are studied in multi-agent settings; see, e.g., \cite*{syrgkanis2017inference for auctions and \cite{magnolfi2019estimation for entry games.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: To develop preference estimates robust to informational assumptions, many of these papers rely on the concept of Bayes correlated equilibrium (\bce), developed by \cite{bergemann2016bayes for games and \cite{kamenica2011bayesian in single-agent settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Given a primitive payoff structure, \bce\ provides a framework for rationalizing outcomes as the result of non-cooperative play without specifying the agents' information structure.
elements.sent-2411-016: Fixed a payoff structure\textemdash namely, agents' utility functions, and their common prior over states\textemdash the analyst can rationalize the outcome distribution as if the players had access to information before playing the game if and only if the outcome distribution is a \bce.
elements.sent-2411-017: Notably, checking whether an outcome is a \bce\ for a given payoff structure requires verifying a finite system of linear inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-018: Repeating this exercise across all possible payoff structures envisioned by the analyst, this procedure characterizes the identified set: it is the set of payoff structures for which the outcome distribution is a \bce.
elements.sent-2411-019: A gap exists, however, between what \bce\ regards as an outcome and the data empirical researchers often have at hand.
elements.sent-2411-020: Indeed, \bce\ presumes the analyst is given a joint distribution over payoff relevant states and action profiles.
elements.sent-2411-021: For instance, suppose the analyst wants to understand whether a judge's decision to grant bail is predicated on information about the likelihood of recidivism.
elements.sent-2411-022: To apply the notion of \bce, the analyst would need to observe the frequency with which the judge grants bail conditional on whether the defendant would recidivate.\footnote{Whereas state-dependent stochastic choice data is useful to guide the design and interpretation of experiments, this data is oftentimes hard to come by outside the experimental setting.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Oftentimes, however, the analyst's data set is more limited.
elements.sent-2411-024: For instance, the analyst may only observe the frequency with which the judge grants bail.
elements.sent-2411-025: More generally, the analyst observes the distribution over action choices.
elements.sent-2411-026: Thus, given a payoff structure, the analyst tests whether a joint distribution over actions and states exists that is a \bce\ and whose marginal over the actions matches the data.
elements.sent-2411-027: Rather than checking a finite system of inequalities, the analyst potentially needs to consider the universe of \bce\ before deciding whether a payoff structure belongs in the identified set.
elements.sent-2411-028: The goal of this paper is to close the above gap in single-agent settings by characterizing the set of marginal distributions over states and actions that are consistent with a \bce\ for a given utility function.
elements.sent-2411-029: Formally, we study the following problem.
elements.sent-2411-030: Fix the ingredients of a decision problem under uncertainty, defined by a set of states, a set of actions, the decision maker's utility as a function of the action she takes and the state of the world, and the decision maker's beliefs over the state of the world.
elements.sent-2411-031: The analyst knows the decision problem and has access to data in the form of a distribution over actions.
elements.sent-2411-032: Given the decision problem, the analyst wants to know whether the action distribution can be rationalized as if the decision maker has access to information before optimally taking her actions.
elements.sent-2411-033: In other words, the analyst wants to know if a \bce\ exists whose marginals over states and actions match the decision maker's prior and the action data.
elements.sent-2411-034: When such a \bce\ exists, we say that the marginals\textemdash i.e., the decision maker's prior and the data\textemdash are \bce-consistent given the utility function.
elements.sent-2411-035: For a given utility function, the set of \bce-consistent marginals identifies the set of priors such that the data is consistent with information.
elements.sent-2411-036: Repeating this exercise across the different utility functions envisioned by the analyst results in the identified set of prior beliefs and utility functions such that the data is consistent with information.
elements.sent-2411-037: Fixed the marginal over actions\textemdash the data\textemdash and the utility function, the inequalities characterize the support function of the set of priors that together with the marginal over the actions are \bce-consistent given the utility function.
elements.sent-2411-038: This observation is useful for two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-039: First, support function methods are popular in empirical work because of the properties enjoyed by the support function (see \citealp{molchanov2018random for a textbook treatment).}
elements.sent-2411-040: Second, our characterization suggests a specific order in which to carry out the identification exercise: For a given utility function, one first characterizes all priors such that the prior and action distribution are \bce-consistent; this exercise is then repeated for each utility function the analyst envisions.
elements.sent-2411-041: This, for instance, gives the analyst more information than if ex ante they had restricted the prior to a parametrized family.
elements.sent-2411-042: While the characterization in \autoref{theorem:h-representation precisely identifies the finitely many inequalities that must be verified in order for the marginals to be \bce-consistent, the characterization is rather implicit in that it does not describe them in closed-form.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The rest of our results consider assumptions on the cardinality of the states or the utility function under which we can express the system of finitely many inequalities in closed-form: \autoref{proposition:2-3 provides a closed form characterization of the system of inequalities when the cardinality of the states is at most $3$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: These inequalities have an immediate economic interpretation: a subset of them verifies that satisfying the martingale property of beliefs is feasible.
elements.sent-2411-045: As we discuss in the main text, the remaining inequalities verify an analogous property for the utility function.

elements.sent-2411-085: In lieu of an organizational paragraph, we collect here mathematical notation and definitions used throughout the paper:.
elements.sent-2411-086: For a finite set \gfset, we denote by $\reals^\gfset$ the set of vectors with dimension $|\gfset|$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Depending on context, we refer to elements of $\reals^\gfset$ either as functions from $\gfset$ to the reals or as vectors in $\reals^\gfset$.
elements.sent-2411-088: We reserve the serif notation $\gelement$ for the function $\gelement:\gfset\rightarrow\reals$ and the boldface notation $\gbold$ for the vector in $\reals^\gfset$.
elements.sent-2411-089: If $\gbold,\gboldb$ are two vectors in $\reals^\gfset$, we denote by $\gbold\gboldb$ their inner product, $\sum_{i=1 ^{|\gfset|}\gbold_\tindex\gboldb_\tindex$.}
elements.sent-2411-090: Given two subsets $\gsubset,\gsubsetb\subset\reals^\gfset$, their Minkowski sum is the set $\gsubset+\gsubsetb=\{\gbold+\gboldb:\gbold\in\gsubset,\gboldb\in\gsubsetb\}$.
elements.sent-2411-091: Given a set $\gsubset\in\reals^\gfset$, the support function of \gsubset\ is the mapping $\pricev\mapsto\sup\{\pricev\gbold:\gbold\in V\}$.
elements.sent-2411-092: A cone \cone\ is a subset of $\reals^\gfset$ such that if $\gbold\in\cone$, then $\alpha\gbold\in\cone$ for all $\alpha\geq0$.
elements.sent-2411-093: A vector $\gbold\in\cone$ is an extreme ray if no linearly independent $\gboldb,\boldsymbol{w}\in\cone$ and positive scalars $\lambda,\gamma$ exist such that $\gbold=\lambda\gboldb+\gamma\boldsymbol{w}$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Note that if $\gbold\in\cone$ is an extreme ray, then so is $\lambda\gbold$ for $\lambda>0$.
elements.sent-2411-095: When we refer to an extreme ray, we refer to one representative of this equivalence class.
elements.sent-2411-096: Finally, a polytope is a subset of $\reals^\gfset$ defined as the intersection of finitely many half-spaces of the form $\bdirection_l\gbold\leq\vol_l$ for some $(\bdirection_l,\vol_l)\in\reals^{\gfset}\times\reals$, $l\in\{1,\dots,L\}$.
elements.paragraph-2411-003: A decision problem with given marginals
elements.sent-2411-097: Our baseline model considers a decision maker (henceforth, \dm) taking an action under uncertainty about a state of the world.
elements.sent-2411-098: We denote by \Types\ a finite set of states of the world and by \Actions\ a finite set of actions.
elements.sent-2411-099: The \dm's utility function $\payoff:\Actions\times\Types\rightarrow\reals$ describes the \dm's payoff as a function of the action she takes and the state of the world.
elements.sent-2411-100: We denote the \dm's prior over \Types\ by $\prior\in\Posteriors$.
elements.sent-2411-101: The \dm's utility defines the set of beliefs for which a given action $\aaction\in\Actions$ is optimal, \opta, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:opt-a} \opta=\left\{\belief\in\Posteriors:(\forall\aactionb\in\Actions)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type)\left(\payoff(\aaction,\type)-\payoff(\aactionb,\type)\right)\geq0\right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-102: We take the viewpoint of an analyst who knows the decision problem, but not whether the \dm\ has access to further information before taking her action.
elements.sent-2411-103: The analyst also observes the \dm's distribution over actions, $\marginal\in\Delta(\Actions)$.
elements.sent-2411-104: The analyst's goal is to determine whether \marginal\ can be rationalized as the result of the \dm\ optimally choosing her actions after observing the outcome of an information structure.
elements.sent-2411-105: The results in \cite{myerson1982optimal,kamenica2011bayesian,bergemann2016bayes imply the analyst's question can be answered by checking whether given the utility function, \payoff, the pair of distributions \pair\ satisfy the following:.}
elements.sent-2411-106: In other words, an information structure exists that rationalizes the \dm's choices \marginal\ given her utility function \payoff\ and prior beliefs \prior\ if and only if a joint distribution $\joint\in\Delta(\Actions\times\Types)$ exists that satisfies Equations \ref{eq:obedience , \ref{eq:state-mg , and \ref{eq:action-mg .}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Equations \ref{eq:state-mg and \ref{eq:action-mg state the joint distribution is consistent with the pair \pair: The generated information averages out to the prior \eqref{eq:state-mg and the \dm's average choices coincide with \marginal\ \eqref{eq:action-mg .}}}}

elements.paragraph-2411-004: The action marginal as a distribution over posteriors
elements.sent-2411-118: A joint distribution $\joint\in\Delta(\Actions\times\Types)$ with marginals $\pair$ induces a belief system, $\{\belief(\cdot|\aaction)\in\Posteriors:\aaction\in\Actions\}$, describing the \dm's beliefs conditional on action \aaction, which satisfies that for all actions $\aaction\in\Actions$, \begin{equation}\marginal(\aaction)\belief(\type|\aaction)=\joint(\aaction,\type).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: In this case, one can view \marginal\ as a distribution over posteriors and the belief system $\left(\belief(\cdot|\aaction)\right)_{\aaction\in\Actions}$ as its support.
elements.sent-2411-120: Consequently, whether \pair\ is \bce-consistent given \payoff\ is equivalent to whether a belief system $\left(\belief(\cdot|\aaction)\right)_{\aaction\in\Actions}$ exists that satisfies the following.
elements.sent-2411-121: First, for all states $\type\in\Types$ \begin{equation}\label{eq:state-mg-b}\tag{BP$_{\prior}$} \sum_{\aaction\in\Actions}\marginal(\aaction)\belief(\type|\aaction)=\prior(\type),\end{equation} and for all $\aaction,\aactionb\in\Actions$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:obedience-b}\tag{O$_\belief$} \sum_{\type\in\Types}\marginal(\aaction)\belief(\type|\aaction)\left[\payoff(\aaction,\type)-\payoff(\aactionb,\type)\right]\geq0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-122: Then, Equations \ref{eq:state-mg-b and \ref{eq:obedience-b require that (i) \marginal\ induces a Bayes plausible distribution over posteriors and (ii) for all actions \aaction, the posterior belief $\belief(\cdot|\aaction)$ is an element of $\opta$.}}

elements.sent-2411-134: Because the set of priors \prior\ such that \pair\ is \bce-consistent given \payoff\ is the Minkowski sum of the polytopes \opta, it follows that the set \ppmpair\ is itself a polytope.
elements.sent-2411-135: As such, it can be represented either via its extreme points (the so-called $V$-representation) or via the half-spaces whose intersection defines it (the so-called $H$-representation).
elements.sent-2411-136: In fact, letting $\ext(\gfset)$ denote the set of extreme points of a subset \gfset\ of $\realst$, we have that \begin{equation}\label{eq:extreme-ms}\tag{$V$-rep} \ext(\minkowski(\payoff,\marginal))\subset\sum_{\aaction\in\Actions}\marginal(\aaction)\ext(\opta).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-137: In words, any extreme point of \ppmpair\ is a \marginal-weighted combination of extreme points in the sets \opta, but the opposite may not hold.
elements.sent-2411-138: In the rest of the paper, we instead focus on the $H$-representation of the set \ppmpair.
elements.sent-2411-139: First, the $V$-representation of the set \ppmpair\ presumes the closed-form characterization of the extreme points of the sets \opta, which in general is not available, except perhaps for the work of \cite{bergemann2015limits .\footnote{In fact, obtaining the $V$-representation of the set \opta\ from its $H$-representation in \autoref{eq:opt-a is known to be a computationally complex problem (cf.. \citealp{weibel2007minkowski ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: Second, most algorithms for computing a Minkowski sum of polytopes rely on the $V$-representation of the polytopes \opta.
elements.sent-2411-141: Moreover, most algorithms add up two sets, say $\opt(\aaction_1)$ and $\opt(\aaction_2)$, and hence, the computation of \ppmpair\ necessitates the $V$-representation of the sum $\opt(\aaction_1)+\opt(\aaction_2)$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-142: Finally, our characterization of the $H$-representation of the set \ppmpair\ relies on the support function of this set, an object that has received increasing attention in the econometrics literature for its nice properties \citep{molchanov2018random .}
elements.sent-2411-143: Because the set \ppmpair\ is convex, it can be characterized via its support function \citep{rockafellar1970convex .}
elements.sent-2411-144: Moreover, the support function of \ppmpair\ is the \marginal-weighted sum of the support function of the sets \opta.
elements.sent-2411-145: Together, these arguments lead to the following statement, which we record for future reference: \begin{observation [Support function of \ppmpair] The prior \prior\ is an element of \ppmpair\ if and only if for all directions $\bdirection\in\realst$, the following holds: \begin{equation}\label{eq:support} \max_{\beliefv\in\ppmpair}\pricev\beliefv=\sum_{\aaction\in\Actions}\marginal(\aaction)\max_{\belief\in\opta}\bdirection\bbelief\geq\bdirection\prior.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-146: The left hand side of \autoref{eq:support represents the support function of the set \ppmpair\ via the support functions of the sets \opta.\footnote{Whereas the result in \autoref{observation:ms obtains directly from the properties of the Minkowski sum, it also follows immediately from the dual representation of the system of equations that define the set \bceu\ in \autoref{definition:bce-c via Farkas' lemma and also from \citet[Theorem 3]{strassen1965existence .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-147: An immediate consequence of \autoref{observation:ms is that to define the $H$-representation of \ppmpair\textemdash defined by a collection of normal vector-height pairs $(\pricev_i,b_i)_{i\in I}$\textemdash the normal vectors alone suffice.}
elements.sent-2411-148: By \autoref{eq:support , the height of the hyperplane determined by the normal vector \pricev\ is the \marginal-weighted sum of the values of the support function of the sets \opta\ in direction \pricev.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The results that follow refine the result in \autoref{observation:ms by describing sets of test functions $\Prices\subsetneq\realst$ such that if \autoref{eq:support holds for all vectors in $\pricev\in\Prices$, then it holds for all vectors $\pricev\in\realst$.\footnote{Ideally, one would like to obtain the minimal $H$-representation of the set \ppmpair.}}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Whereas the results in Theorems \ref{theorem:h-representation through \ref{proposition:2-step provide the minimal-$H$ representation of \ppmpair\ in some environments, we use the term test functions because these vectors are oftentimes a superset of the minimal-$H$ representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: A straightforward implication from the Minkowski sum representation of the set \ppmpair\ is that the face structure of the sets \opta\ determine that of the set \ppmpair, and hence its $H$-representation.
elements.sent-2411-152: The concepts of the normal fan of a polytope and the common refinement of the normal fans of a collection of polytopes, to which we turn next, allow us to describe how the face structure of the sets \opta\ determine the $H$-representation of the set \ppmpair:.

elements.sent-2411-154: The normal fan of \polytope, $\normal(\polytope)$, is the collection of the cones $\normal_\face$ over the faces of \polytope.

elements.sent-2411-197: To provide intuition for \autoref{eq:bce-c-actions , considering the binary action case is useful.}
elements.sent-2411-199: In that case, \bce-consistency of \pair\ implies a belief system exists that satisfies at most two obedience constraints, which we can write as the following chain of inequalities: \begin{equation}\marginal(\aaction_1)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type|\aaction_1)\left[\payoff(\aaction_1,\type)-\payoff(\aaction_2,\type)\right]\geq0\geq\marginal(\aaction_2)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type|\aaction_2)\left[\payoff(\aaction_1,\type)-\payoff(\aaction_2,\type)\right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-200: That is, information resulting in beliefs $\belief(\cdot|\aaction_1)$ and $\belief(\cdot|\aaction_2)$ alters the relative ranking of $\aaction_1$ and $\aaction_2$.
elements.sent-2411-201: Equation \ref{eq:state-mg-b implies that if we add up both sides of the above chain, we obtain the following: \begin{equation}\marginal(\aaction_1)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type|\aaction_1)\left[\payoff(\aaction_1,\type)-\payoff(\aaction_2,\type)\right]+\marginal(\aaction_2)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type|\aaction_2)\left[\payoff(\aaction_1,\type)-\payoff(\aaction_2,\type)\right]= = \sum_{\type\in\Types}\prior(\type)\left[\payoff(\aaction_1,\type)-\payoff(\aaction_2,\type)\right].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-202: In other words, while information can alter the relative ranking between the two actions, it cannot systematically do so: On average, the ranking between $\aaction_1$ and $\aaction_2$ must coincide with how the \dm\ ranks these two actions at the prior \prior.
elements.sent-2411-203: This is the analogue to the belief martingale condition, albeit in terms of the \dm's payoffs, and hence we refer to it as a payoff martingale condition.
elements.sent-2411-204: Using once again the property that $\belief(\cdot|\aaction)\in\opta$, the above equality implies \autoref{eq:bce-c-actions .}

elements.sent-2411-206: Whereas the obedience constraints feature the comparison between these two actions when $\aaction_1$ or $\aaction_2$ is recommended, no such inequality arises when $\aaction_3$ is recommended.

elements.sent-2411-208: The reason is that when ensuring that $\aaction_3$ is optimal, the obedience constraints place no restrictions on the relative ranking of $\aaction_1$ and $\aaction_2$.
elements.sent-2411-209: Yet, the ranking of these two actions has to average to their ranking at the prior over all beliefs the \dm\ has.

elements.section-2411-004: \bce-consistency in monotone and concave decision problems
elements.sent-2411-246: In this section, we identify assumptions on the utility function \payoff, which allow us to refine the basic characterization in \autoref{theorem:h-representation without imposing assumptions on the cardinality of the state space.}
elements.sent-2411-247: Concretely, we focus on monotone and concave decision problems in which the utility function \payoff\ satisfies concavity and increasing differences assumptions (\autoref{assumption:mcv ).}
elements.sent-2411-248: We next consider two special cases: affine utility differences (\autoref{sec:affine ) and two-step utility differences (\autoref{sec:two-step ).}}
elements.sent-2411-249: In each case, we characterize the set of \bce-consistent marginals via a system of finitely many inequalities.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Comparative statics with affine utility differences
elements.sent-2411-367: Under the assumption of affine utility differences, we consider in this section changes to the prior or the utility function that preserve the rationalization of a given marginal distribution over actions.
elements.sent-2411-368: Suppose \pair\ is \bce-consistent given \payoff\ and let \priorb\ denote another prior belief.
elements.sent-2411-369: When can we say that $(\priorb,\marginal)$ are \bce-consistent given \payoff? The following definition is key:.
elements.sent-2411-370: Note that $\prior\circ\diff^{-1}$ is the distribution of payoffs\textemdash measured by the utility difference \diff\textemdash faced by the \dm\ under the prior distribution \prior.
elements.sent-2411-371: When \priorb\ is a mean preserving spread of \prior, the \dm\ faces a riskier payoff distribution under \priorb\ than under \prior.
elements.sent-2411-372: Conversely, we can obtain the payoff distribution under \prior\ by a garbling of the payoff distribution under \priorb.
elements.sent-2411-373: A fortiori, if \pair\ is \bce-consistent given \payoff, then so is $(\priorb,\marginal)$.
elements.sent-2411-374: Suppose \payoff\ satisfies affine utility differences and that \pair\ is \bce-consistent given \payoff.
elements.sent-2411-375: Then, if \priorb\ is a \diff-mean preserving spread of \prior, $(\priorb,\marginal)$ is \bce-consistent given \payoff.
elements.sent-2411-376: The result follows from the shape of the test functions $\Prices_{\aud}$ and noting that if \priorb\ is a \diff-mean preserving spread of \prior, then for all \candt, $\pricevup_{\candt}\priorb\leq\pricevup_{\candt}\prior$ and $\pricevdown_{\candt}\priorb\leq\pricevdown_{\candt}\prior$.

elements.sent-2411-457: A ring-network game is a base game \base\ in which player's payoffs satisfy the following: \begin{equation}\label{eq:rn-game}\tag{RN-P} \payoff_1(\aaction,\type) =\rnpayoff_1(\aaction_1,\type) (\forall \playerindex\geq2)\payoffi(\aaction,\type) =\rnpayoff_\playerindex(\aaction_{\playerindex-1},\actioni).\nonumber\end{equation} In words, player $1$ cares about their action and the state of the world, whereas for $\playerindex\geq2$ player \playerindex\ cares about their action and that of player $\playerindex-1$.

elements.sent-2411-468: Given $\pair$ and \payoff, we characterize in this section the set of distribution over posteriors (if any) that rationalize the \dm's distribution over actions.
elements.sent-2411-469: For a distribution over posteriors \bsplit\ to rationalize \marginal, two conditions must be satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-470: First, the mean of \bsplit\ is equal to the prior, \prior; that is, \bsplit\ must be Bayes' plausible.
elements.sent-2411-471: Second, the distribution over posteriors \bsplit\ must induce the distribution over actions \marginal.
elements.sent-2411-472: Formally, let $\aaction^*(\belief)$ denote the \dm's optimal set of actions when their belief is \belief.
elements.sent-2411-473: Then, \bsplit\ induces \marginal\ if a decision rule $\strat:\Posteriors\mapsto\Delta(\Actions)$ exists such that for every $\aaction\in\Actions$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:demand-supply}\marginal(\aaction)=\int\strat(\belief)(\aaction)\bsplit(d\belief),\end{equation} and $\aaction\in\text{ supp }\strat(\belief)(\cdot)$ only if $\aaction\in\aaction^*(\belief)$.
elements.sent-2411-474: Whereas in the previous section we interpreted the marginal distribution \marginal\ as a Bayes plausible distribution over posteriors, \autoref{eq:demand-supply shows this analogy is perhaps incomplete.}

elements.sent-2411-479: That \bsplit\ implements \marginal\ is equivalent to being able to allocate the supply of beliefs to satisfy the action demands in a market clearing way.

elements.sent-2411-481: Building on the main theorem in that paper, \autoref{proposition:core-bp below characterizes when \bsplit\ implements \marginal.}
elements.sent-2411-482: To state \autoref{proposition:core-bp , one final piece of notation is needed.}
elements.sent-2411-483: Given a Bayes plausible $\bsplit\in\Delta(\Posteriors)$, we construct a measure over subsets \Actionsb\ of the set of actions \Actions\ as follows.
elements.sent-2411-484: For each $\Actionsb\subseteq\Actions$, define the push-forward measure $\bsplit_\Actions(\Actionsb)$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:tau-consideration-sets} \bsplit_\Actions(\Actionsb)=\bsplit\left(\left[\aaction^*\right]^{-1}(\Actionsb)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-485: In words, each action subset \Actionsb\ has mass equal to the probability that \bsplit\ induces a belief under which \Actionsb\ is optimal.
elements.sent-2411-487: A Bayes plausible distribution over posteriors, $\bsplit\in\Delta(\Posteriors)$, implements $\marginal$ if and only if the following holds \begin{equation}\label{eq:core} (\forall\Actionsb\subseteq\Actions)\sum_{\aaction\in\Actionsb}\marginal(\aaction)\geq\sum_{\Actionsc\subseteq\Actionsb}\bsplit_\Actions(\Actionsc).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-501: We now draw a connection with stochastic choice out of menus which, among other things, allows us to illustrate why \autoref{eq:core implies \bsplit\ implements \marginal.}

elements.sent-2411-506: Consider now the following ``experiment'': We first draw a menu $\Actionsb$ using $\bsplit_\Actions$ and then draw an action $\aaction\in\Actionsb$ according to $\scr(\cdot|\Actionsb)$.
elements.sent-2411-507: We only inform the \dm\ of the drawn action, but not the menu from which it was drawn.
elements.sent-2411-508: Because we draw menu \Actionsb\ only when it is the optimal set of actions, we only recommend \aaction\ when it is optimal for the \dm\ to follow the recommendation.
elements.sent-2411-509: As we explain below, \autoref{eq:stochastic-menu-choice then implies that this ``experiment'' induces the \dm\ to take actions with the desired frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-510: Technically, we have not described an experiment\textemdash a collection of signal distributions conditional on the state of the world\textemdash but this can be done immediately as follows: For each $\type\in\Types$ and $\aaction\in\Actions$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:state-dependent-stochastic-choice} \tilde{\scr}(\aaction|\type)=\sum_{\Actionsb:\aaction\in\Actionsb}\sum_{\{\belief\insupp\;\bsplit:\aaction^*(\belief)=\Actionsb\}}\frac{\belief(\type)}{\prior(\type)}\bsplit(\belief)\scr(\aaction|\Actionsb).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-511: In this experiment, the \dm\ receives an action recommendation conditional on the state of the world, so that \autoref{eq:state-dependent-stochastic-choice describes the \dm's state-dependent stochastic choice.}
elements.sent-2411-512: The previous discussion connects two sets of conditional distribution over choices that arise in the stochastic choice literature: stochastic choices conditional on a state of the world (\autoref{eq:state-dependent-stochastic-choice ) and stochastic choices out of a menu\textemdash$\scr$ in \autoref{eq:stochastic-menu-choice .}}

elements.sent-2411-700: An analyst observes the frequency with which a decision maker (\dm) takes actions, but not the frequency conditional on payoff-relevant states.
elements.sent-2411-701: We ask when the analyst can rationalize the \dm's choices as if the \dm\ first learns something about the state before acting.
elements.sent-2411-702: We provide a support-function characterization of the triples of utility functions, prior beliefs, and (marginal) distributions over actions such that the \dm's action distribution is consistent with information given the agent's prior and utility function.
elements.sent-2411-703: We apply our characterization to study comparative statics and to identify conditions under which a single information structure rationalizes choices across multiple decision problems.
elements.sent-2411-704: We extend our results to settings with a continuum of actions and states assuming the first-order approach applies, and to simple multi-agent settings.
elements.sent-2411-705: JEL codes: D44, D82, D83 Keywords: revealed preference, revealed information, distributions with given marginals, support function, stochastic choice out of menus, state-dependent stochastic choice, information design, Bayesian persuasion, flows in networks, optimal transport \pagenumbering{arabic.}
elements.sent-2411-706: When economic agents make decisions under uncertainty, they rely on information about unknown factors.
elements.sent-2411-707: Yet, researchers rarely observe this information directly, creating a fundamental challenge: How can we infer what agents know from their observed choices? This challenge has significant implications for economic modeling, because assumptions about information can dramatically impact model predictions and parameter estimates.
elements.sent-2411-708: In this paper, we provide a framework to determine when observed choice patterns can be rationalized by some information structure, without requiring the researcher to observe the relationship between choices and underlying states.
elements.sent-2411-709: Specifically, given a decision maker's (\dm) utility function, prior beliefs, and an observed distribution of actions, we characterize when this action distribution can be explained as the result of the \dm\ optimally responding to some information about the state.
elements.sent-2411-710: Our main contribution is a support-function characterization that translates this question into a finite system of inequalities involving the utility function, prior, and action distribution.
elements.sent-2411-711: Consider a concrete example: an analyst studying whether a judge's bail decisions are informed by recidivism risk \citep{rambachan2022identifying .}
elements.sent-2411-712: The analyst observes only the frequency with which the judge grants bail, not the frequency with which the judge grants bail conditional on whether the defendant will recidivate.
elements.sent-2411-713: Our framework allows the analyst to determine which combinations of the judge's utility function and prior beliefs about recidivism would make the observed bail decisions consistent with the judge having some information about recidivism risk.
elements.sent-2411-714: Many recent empirical studies seek preference estimates robust to informational assumptions, recognizing how strongly these assumptions affect outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-715: For instance, \cite{dickstein2018exporters , \cite*{dickstein2024patient , \cite{gualdani2019identification , and \cite{rambachan2022identifying develop methods to understand the role of information in firms' export decisions, physicians' treatment recommendations, voter choices, and prediction mistakes, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-716: Similar approaches appear in multi-agent settings such as auctions \citep*{syrgkanis2017inference and entry games \citep{magnolfi2019estimation .}}
elements.sent-2411-717: Some of these approaches rely on Bayes correlated equilibrium (\bce), developed by \cite{bergemann2016bayes for games and \cite{kamenica2011bayesian for single-agent settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-718: Given a payoff structure\textemdash players' utility functions and their common prior over states\textemdash\bce\ provides conditions under which an outcome distribution can be rationalized as if players had access to information before playing.
elements.sent-2411-719: Importantly, checking whether an outcome is a \bce\ requires verifying only a finite system of linear inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-720: However, a critical gap exists between what \bce\ requires and the data empirical researchers typically have available.
elements.sent-2411-721: In our judge example, \bce\ would require observing bail decisions conditional on whether defendants would recidivate\textemdash data that are rarely available.
elements.sent-2411-722: In practice, analysts typically observe only the marginal distribution of actions (the frequency of bail grants), not the action distribution conditional on the state (the frequency of bail grants conditional on recidivism risk).
elements.sent-2411-723: For a given payoff structure, rather than checking finitely many linear inequalities, the analyst checks whether a \bce\ exists whose marginals over the actions matches the observed choices.
elements.sent-2411-724: Our paper bridges this gap in single-agent settings by characterizing when marginal distributions over states and actions are consistent with a (single-agent) \bce\ given the \dm's utility function.
elements.sent-2411-725: Formally, given the triple of a utility function, prior beliefs, and observed action distribution, we study when a \bce\ exists whose marginals over the states and actions coincide with the \dm's prior and action distribution.
elements.sent-2411-726: When such a \bce\ exists, we say the marginals\textemdash the \dm's prior and the observed action distribution\textemdash are \bce-consistent given the utility function.
elements.sent-2411-727: Our main contributions are in Sections \ref{sec:main and \ref{sec:utility .}}
elements.sent-2411-728: In \autoref{theorem:h-representation , we provide a characterization in terms of a system of finitely many inequalities, linear in both marginal distributions, such that the marginals are \bce-consistent if and only if these inequalities hold.}

elements.sent-2411-743: Our results have implications for both empirical work and theoretical analysis.
elements.sent-2411-744: Empirically, for a given action distribution and utility function, our characterization results non-parametrically identify the set of priors such that the prior and action distribution are \bce-consistent given the utility function, which is useful whenever the analyst has no information on what the prior should be, but may have auxiliary data on the \dm’s payoffs.\footnote{By contrast, studies of risk aversion across domains assume the \dm's beliefs about expected claim rates coincide with the frequencies in the data and estimate the curvature of the utility function \citep*{cohen2007estimating, barseghyan2011risk,barseghyan2013nature .}}
elements.sent-2411-745: Furthermore, for a given action distribution, our results characterize joint restrictions on the prior and the utility function for the triple of the utility function, prior, and action distribution to be consistent with information.
elements.sent-2411-746: Our framework also has applications in behavioral economics, particularly for cognitive uncertainty models where $\mathrm{DM}$s exhibit random behavior across instances of the same problem (e.g., \citealp*{khaw2021cognitive ; \citealp{enke2023cognitive ).}}
elements.sent-2411-747: In these models, the state often represents the correct action and the \dm\ has a noisy perception of this state.
elements.sent-2411-748: Whereas laboratory experiments may generate state-dependent choice data, outside the lab, analysts typically only observe average choices.
elements.sent-2411-749: Our results can test whether behavior is consistent with Bayesian cognitive uncertainty\textemdash whether noisy perception of states can be rationalized via an information structure.
elements.sent-2411-750: Theoretically, our results open up the study of marginal information design\textemdash akin to reduced-form implementation in mechanism design\textemdash where an information designer cares only about the \dm's actions, and not the state of the world.
elements.sent-2411-751: From this perspective, results such as \autoref{proposition:mcv reveal the structure of the binding constraints in information design problems, and we expect it can be used to further the study of Bayesian persuasion.}

elements.sent-2411-775: Our model considers a \dm\ taking an action under uncertainty about a state of the world.
elements.sent-2411-776: We denote by $\Types=\{\type_1,\dots,\type_\tnum\}$ the finite set of states of the world and by $\Actions=\{\aaction_1,\dots,\aaction_{\anum}\}$ the finite set of actions.
elements.sent-2411-777: The tuple $\langle\Types,\Actions,\payoff\rangle$ defines the decision problem.
elements.sent-2411-778: Letting \Posteriors\ denote the set of distributions over \Types, the \dm's utility defines the set of beliefs for which a given action $\aaction\in\Actions$ is optimal, \opta, as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:opt-a} \opta=\{\belief\in\Posteriors:(\forall\aactionb\in\Actions)\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type)\left(\payoff(\aaction,\type)-\payoff(\aactionb,\type)\right)\geq0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-779: Below, we regard \Posteriors\ as a full-dimensional set in $\reals^{\tnum-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-780: Furthermore, to streamline the presentation, we implicitly assume each nonempty \opta\ is full dimensional in $\reals^{\tnum-1}$.\footnote{Because $\cup_{\aaction\in\Actions}\opta=\Posteriors$, at least one \opta\ is full dimensional in $\reals^{\tnum-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-781: Assuming all nonempty \opta\ are full-dimensional sets in $\reals^{\tnum-1}$ simplifies exposition.
elements.sent-2411-782: Thus, whenever we refer to the dimension of a set, we mean its dimension in $\reals^{\tnum-1}$, even if our notation does not make it explicit.

elements.sent-2411-815: Although \autoref{eq:minkowski-representation characterizes the set of \bce-consistent distributions for a fixed utility function \payoff\ and action distribution \marginal, it does not capture the joint restrictions on the prior and utility that arise from requiring \marginal\ to be rationalizable via information.}

elements.sent-2411-819: In the remainder of the paper, we focus on studying the $H$-representation of the set \ppmpair.

elements.sent-2411-839: The normal vectors defining the minimal $H$-representation of a full-dimensional polytope correspond to the extreme rays of the one-dimensional cones in the normal fan of that polytope \citep{ziegler2012lectures .}

elements.sent-2411-858: The pair $\pair\in\Posteriors\times\Delta(\Actions)$ is \bce-consistent given \payoff\ if and only if \autoref{eq:support holds for all $\pricev\in\Prices_{\opt}$, that is, if and only if for all states $\type\in\Types$, \begin{equation}\sum_{\aaction\in\Actions}\marginal(\aaction)\min_{\belief\in\opta}\belief(\type) \leq\prior(\type),\label{eq:bce-c-states}\tag{BM} \intertext{and for all pairs of actions $\aactionb\in\Actions_+$ and $\aactionbb\in\Actions$,} \label{eq:bce-c-actions} \sum_{\aaction\in\Actions}\marginal(\aaction)\max_{\belief\in\opta}\sum_{\type\in\Types}\belief(\type)\left[\payoff(\aactionbb,\type)-\payoff(\aactionb,\type)\right] \geq\sum_{\type\in\Types}\prior(\type)\left[\payoff(\aactionbb,\type)-\payoff(\aactionb,\type)\right].\tag{PM}\end{equation}.}
